speaker: interviewer
text: Mike Gluck in Los Angeles. Go ahead, Mike.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after that, we got up in the morning, we stood in an appell, and then they gave us a little coffee and a slice of bread. And they told us to stay in line five in a row, and we were marching. Where, we didn't know the first day where we're going. Then they took us in 187 or 185 steps down. That was our-- a stone mine, whatever they called it. And then one SS told us that-- to pick up a stone and to keep that stone between our legs, between our feet, and to wait. So each one went there and picked up a stone. And the meantime, the SS men went into the office and they had a cup of coffee, you know, and then we-- we picked up all our stones, and then we waited with our hands in the back. And he came and he checked if we-- each one took us enough big stone, you know, not-- not if we've committed sin, we take a small stone. We have to take a big stone. And whoever he didn't like, he says, you didn't take a big stone. You didn't take enough big stone. You come with me! He took him to the, uh, pile of stones, and he made him pick up a stone maybe bigger than he was. And he couldn't pick it up. So what happened is, come here! There was the guard on top in that little booth there, and he says, just like that. Shot him! Just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Death right away. We-- and then we went unload the stones, now bring back the stones. They didn't know what to do. So again, the same thing for about four, five times a day. And there was every day in that group, and we were about, uh, twe-- 15 or 20 people. There was every day, four, five people got shot. Every day. So finally after six days being in Ausch-- in, uh, Mauthausen, so they made us again line up in-- in appell. And they took us. They made us-- they took us to the station, to the train station, and took us to the camp by the name Gusen Zwei. This is Gusen Two. It was Gusen One. It was Gusen Zwei.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like a satellite camp out of Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, from Mauthausen. It was about four or five kilometers from Mauthausen. And they put me in a barrack with a lot of other people. And then that Gusen Zwei was just like a slaughterhouse. They had our, uh, uh-- a specific amount they have to kill every day. The SS, the kapos, all of them, they were after it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one, I was in a block one-- that block that there was a hook up in the ceiling-- like this, a hook. From the hook, there was hanging down a cord. At the end of the cord there was a little stick this big. He tied that cord in the middle of that hook. And then he came in and out and we went to sleep-- so-called sleep. Five, six people, a little thing that's not enough even for one person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he had a good dinner or what, he came in like he is god. He owns the world. He came in and he picked anybody he wanted from it. Come here! Lay down! Here, the poor man lay down. And he put that stick that he held in his hand on his neck, and he stepped on the both side of the stick. And he hold himself on that hook with that stick too. And he danced on him for about five minutes till he killed him. And this was going on day by day, night after night. And that was-- and that was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No work in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was working. I was working the beton kommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this [INAUDIBLE] kommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that camp, yeah. But-- but that's-- that was his pleasure, to come and choke a few people every night.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is after you came back from work?

speaker: interviewee
text: From work, after we came back from work. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you after you came back from work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what they gave me! They gave me-- they gave us a little-- little water there and a-- and a piece of sliced bread in order, you know, to-- to just to survive, not to live. And also after work, my-- we were working the Messerschmitt kommando. And there were people who were working with the Messerschmitt, and we were working in the beton kommando to make the, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: The cement.

speaker: interviewee
text: --cement. And-- and we were there for nine months like this here. It was just you-- every day you saw there were the people, the inmates, the people who came and-- and schlepp a wagon of three-- three, four wheels and loaded up the dead. Loaded up the dead, every day, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mike, I have to interrupt you. Your uncle was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My uncle-- I'm sorry I didn't s-- I didn't. We were-- we were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we track back?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Plazsow, we were separated. He was gone-- where, I don't know. And, uh-- and-- and-- and I didn't know where he-- where he went because we didn't know from each other where we go. And then I, uh-- I want to go back to Gusen Zwei. And-- and there, I lived there for about 12-- for about nine months exactly. And then after-- before there was about 10 or 12 days-- what have I figured out now was that 10 or 12 days-- was before the Americans came in-- they was liberating us-- they were still had this one [INAUDIBLE] that they had the only out of [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE]-- to destroy the few poor little broken up Jews. So they made us stand up because in that Gusen Zwei, there was all nationalities-- Hungarians, Spanish, and-- and this and that. And I want to go back. In Gusen Zwei--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean-- uh, excuse me for a [INAUDIBLE]. By all nationalities, you mean by the SS or by the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there-- what I want to say is why, and even then they had that just one thing they had in mind, just to destroy the few people. And I want to go back now to, uh-- to what-- what-- what kind of a life we had in Gusen Zwei before we left Gusen Zwei. That is, I remember one time if you were sick, they put you in a hos-- so-called in a hospital, in a hospital. And I did not want to go there. I just tried to be whatever I could to stay alive. And one day-- I'll never forget that either. And one day, there was-- they took all the people from the hospital. It was around January or February. It was bitter cold outside. And they took all the people out of the hospital, and they just threw them out on the street, threw them out on the street, bare naked, in a-- in a bitter cold day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean on-- in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp. On the streets in the camp. And they were naked. And I remember I was sitting there by the door like this here in the window, and I saw a man-- I knew the man. He was a very learned man. We used to have sometimes conversation when he was still alive and was still healthy. And he was there and he was begging himself, let me in! We were not allowed to open up the door for nobody. And the way he said, let me in and then he's-- goes quiet and then how he died. And I thought to myself that time, if this man would take-- be taken into a house and given him a warm glass of tea and taken and put in a nice warm bath, he could be still alive and he could still contribute to the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still thinking about your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: But, yeah. And I says, my god, this man should be just in a little-- in a home somewhere warmed up a little bit. And there was hundreds of people like this here on the street in the camp scattered around. And we were not allowed to open up the door. And this is how they died-- naked, frozen, and outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without them being shot. Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, they were not even shooting them. No, they were just-- this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Talking about your home, Mike, I have to track you back. What happened to your sister? She was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was in my home town, and she went with my aunt and uncle to Auschwitz. And there, she went on the-- on the left side with the children. [INTERPOSING VOICES] She was only 14, When we were arrived in Ausch--

speaker: interviewer
text: After [INAUDIBLE]. And then you took your little cousin-- as I'm tracking back-- and hugged her. They took your sister at the same time away with your aunt and her children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the same time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your sister approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was, at that time, around 14. And she didn't-- she-- she could have passed to go on the right side, but she wanted to go with the aunt. I mean she knew I-- we didn't know what's going to happen, so she went on the left side. And she never came back. She'd perished in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as you told me before, your mother died before the war from natural causes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, yes. Natural causes. Yeah, we were orphans. So we were scattered around. I was visiting an uncle, and my-- my sister was with my aunt and uncle. And my uncle was the brother of my-- brother of my mother. So we were together. Anyway. So I, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry for interrupting.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody was dying like flies from the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it  was hundreds of people were laying on the street. And there was not only Jewish people. There was gentiles, too. You know, they were throwing on the street like this here. It didn't matter who. And then the next day, we scattered around all the bodies on that little wagons. We schlepped that wagon into the crematorium. Whatever they did with them, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see a crematorium, Mike?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I did not see it. If I would have seen a crematorium, I probably wouldn't be alive because they didn't have-- want to have any witnesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but I'm meaning when you took those dead bodies--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no we didn't see anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took them to a pile or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't see anything. They made sure we didn't see anything. There shouldn't be any witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the dead bodies?

words.text: What did you do with the

speaker: interviewee
text: We just threw them into the pile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into a pile.

speaker: interviewee
text: Piles and piles. In the daytime when people were dying-- they were killing the people-- we were told to put the bodies like this and like this. They shouldn't fall down. Like this, crossing, crossing all the bodies. And this is how the people came in the morning, collect the bodies from the, uh-- from the-- from the barracks and took them to their destination. So anyway, so, uh-- and then they gave an order to all the Jews to stand in appell, in appell. And that was before the liberation. Before that-- I understood at that time that that was the, uh-- the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any sh-- any bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we heard-- we heard-- yes, we heard a lot. And then they gave us a hot soup, really hot soup. And they gave us five minutes to finish it, and we couldn't eat it because it was too hot. And while we were standing and as soon as we start marching, we were not allowed to have the soup. Throw it away. So we marched from Mauthausen to Gunskirchen for about five or six days. Didn't give us a piece of bread, not a glass of water, nothing. We were just walking like animals. And what we ate-- and we weren't allowed to do that either, but I was watching the SS, should not catch me. We ate the, uh-- the, uh-- whatchamacallit-- the bugs from the grass, on the grass. We ate that in order to stay alive, and I couldn't eat it. People were eating, and they says, eat it! You gotta eat it. So-- so anyway, so, uh-- so we were marching. And one time they were-- there was-- there was, uh-- I don't know the name of that little town. We were passing by--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean to tell me, from that labor camp, from that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gusen Zwei.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Gusen Zwei, they took you on a march.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a march to Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: To destroy us, to kill us all. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know. They just took you on a march.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a march. They took us on a march, and we marched through a little town-- I don't know the name. If I would have knowed the name of that town and I would know, remember where it was, I would have gone back after the war. The reason why, we were marching by, and there was people sitting in the balcony. And on top was a little store, was a little sign up there, Gluck, like my name. And I says to the man there, my name is also Gluck. Maybe you can help me with a piece of bread. [SPEAKING GERMAN] They started to curse them, like [SPEAKING GERMAN] and you have a nerve. So I walked-- I marched--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the people that lived in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that town, yes, in that town. And so we walked by, and I-- we got to the camp. And they put us in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- what was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gunskirchen. That was the plan, to take us in that camp. It was out of everywhere. It was in a-- in a-- in a-- in the woods somewhere, and the barracks were made just [? slotting. ?] The plan was that to kill us all there. But the way I heard after the war, that the Americans find out what they had then planned to do with these few people there in Gunskirchen. So they gave them an order, and each man we're going to find a dead man in that camp, there will be two Germans killed for that. And then they stopped. And they stopped killing us, but they didn't feed us. And then two or three days, we start-- we got-- we started to get a package from the, uh, Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean to tell me they liberated you already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not liberated. We were still under the Germans. Three or four days before the liberation, we got from the Red Cross somehow some little packages. There was beans, not cooked, flour, not cooked. And they gave us a little package, which wasn't enough even for one person, for 28 people. We had to divide between us. 28 people. And I-- I still remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans were still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still there. And I remember there was a young boy. And a German with his gun, pushed and pushed and pushed him like this. What he did, I don't know. He must have been around 17 or 18 year old little boy. And he went on his knees, and he says, please, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means, please, give me-- don't take my life away. Don't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little boy, a Jewish boy, a little boy from the camp. And the SS, shh! Go on your knees. Just shot him, walked away, just like that. And that was only three days before the liberation, for God's sake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that little boy's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I just-- I don't remember that boy. No. I-- this is still in my mind, still in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I stood in the side, and I says, boy, oh, boy, if I could have a knife, I could smash this-- bag that goddamn German. I was so mad. It was just three days before the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but you were so weak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, we-- we were-- we were under the impression--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still had in your mind to kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but we were under the impression that we are nothing. We're haftling. We have no rights. We have no law. We have no nothing. But I still had in my mind, if I would had a knife that time, I would have just gone to back of the goddamn German, and I would slash his back open, in the back if I could have done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you have the energy to do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have done it anyway I could. But I couldn't and I didn't have a knife. I didn't have-- and he just walked away, walked away like nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: After he killed that little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I wanted-- when they-- when they brought us into Gunskirchen, they put us in a room enough for 10 people, maybe 15. They pushed in maybe 150 people in that room. We couldn't stand, and we couldn't sit down. We couldn't lay down. We couldn't breathe. We couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days before the liberation. And the floor was just mud. It was nothing. Let me tell you something. After two days, we had enough room to sleep in that room because everybody died. In fact, I took even a body to have my pillow to sleep on. This is the way-- degraded we were, you know. This is how much degraded we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not the only one. You're not the only one.

words.text: You're not the only one. You're

speaker: interviewee
text: This is how we were degraded. And after-- and I saw what's going on, I says to myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean to tell me the Americans were two days away from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days! That was around Wednesday or Tuesday--

speaker: interviewer
text: And hundreds of people died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands of people died in a few days because they didn't feed us. They put us in a room for-- to-- to-- to choke us completely. So therefore they didn't have to-- didn't have to have no guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sicknesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, yeah, typhuses, typhuses. But after, in 1945, May the 4, I remember in the afternoon, we heard shots. We heard shots. And we didn't know what it was, but we hoped that this is the Americans. And it sure, it was. Boy, they didn't-- nobody shoot into the camp. And those goddamn Germans, all those guards around us, they had civ-- military suits on the top, and civilian clothes underneath the military clothes. And soon as they saw that it's the end, they pulled their military clothes off, threw their g-- their, uh, guns away, and just start running like civilians. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was Friday night. And then before that time, they were serving us a little soup. And I saw a bone there in the soup, you know? And I says to the guy who serves the soup, I says, you know what? I give you the soup, give me that bone from that soup. So he gave me the bone from the soup. And I stood with a stone, and I hacked on the stone. And I ripped up a little bone, and I ate it like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, the shots were coming. The shots were coming. And everybody was screaming, we're frei. We're free. We're free. We're free. And everybody was running to the-- to the warehouse for the bread and something like that. I said to myself, I'm not going to run because a lot of people got killed in that time pushing each other too much. So I saw a guy coming--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they were weak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weak, yeah, but they still want to have a piece of bread. And after the, uh-- I said to myself, I'm not going to go. I'm not going to push myself. If the-- if the freiheit is here, I want to g-- I want to have-- I want to-- I want to be in there, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to be liberated. And then I, uh-- I saw a guy coming here that had three breads. I says, you have three breads. Give me one bread. He gave me one bread. And we ate it, and we were liberated. And we slept in the camp that time. We were afraid to leave. And in the morning, I remember like now, there was-- God bless him-- [SOBS] an American soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Ah, who-- who knew a name? I didn't know my name. And he came and he stood. He pried open those-- those gates. He stood on top of the hood of the jeep, and says, people, you go wherever you want to go. You are free. And we started to scream and cry. And we went out of the camp, was in the woods far away from-- from Wels, right by Wels. It was by Wels. So we walked. As soon as we got into the city--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria. The American soldiers, I'm telling you, they were just like angels. They did so much. They were sweating, and carrying those sick people into the c-- into the hospitals, and sweating. And-- and-- and never it was too much for them to do. And when we walked in-- we walk-- we marched in from the camp into Wels, there was American soldiers just, come, come, come, come. I didn't speak no English. Come, come come. And showed us to go that way. So we went that way so there were nurses. Was a hospital around there. And they-- they made us go up, and the nurses said, come, come, come. We went up there-- all people, a lot of people. And they start to undress us. I had-- I had no hair on my head. I had a stripe here like this. And my-- I didn't realize that time my head, my hair, was this thick with mud. And this I was walking around. They were scraping off my-- the mud from my head for maybe for an hour until they could get it off. They bathed me. They gave me fresh, clean clothes. And they said, you can go anywhere you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- I went around there. I was alone. I was looking in the city, and it comes evening-- Were you able to go? You were not sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- no, I was-- was able to go. I was 19 years old, 20 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was weak. I was no more than 60 pounds, or maybe-- maybe 50, 50, 60 pounds, but my will was just to be liberated, to be able to be free, to be counted like a man. That's what I wanted. And will is more than power sometimes, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, I remember one evening-- the first day, Saturday. I was looking in a window there, and there is a truck stopped in the street. And there is a colored soldier came over, and he says, sleep, sleep, sleep? I didn't know what he meant, so the people from the truck-- also my kind of people-- come on, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They're going to take us to put us to sleep, somewhere to go to sleep. To rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So went on the truck, and they took us to Linz, to Linz. And we stayed there in-- overnight. And then in the morning, I says, I'm not going to stay here. I'm going to go back to Wels. And soon as I walk out from there in the morning, I met-- I found a friend of mine. We were growing up together in the same hometown. His name was Alter. He still lives in-- in New York now. His name is Alter Pollack. And so we stood together like-- like two brothers, and we walked back from Guns-- from Linz into Wels. And then I realized how much American--

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked that much? It was about 20 kilometer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for three or four days. Three or four days we walked. Don't know how many miles.

words.text: walked. Don't know how many miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was about 20, 21 kilometer from Linz to Wels.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked about three days, yeah. So we walked, and every time we walked in a little-- in a house, and we ask-- ask them for food. And they gave us food. I mean-- it was no-- we walked back to, uh-- we walked back to Wels. And we came into the camp. Then I realized what the American soldiers did for us. That-- that time I realized how these soldiers carried those sick people, and how they gave us an order not to eat too much in the beginning. They fed us like just like-- like-- like parents would fed us, you know? Slowly, rice, a little cr-- a little cream of wheat. And they served us the little things until we got to-- to ourself a little bit more and more and more. God bless them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they were experienced already from other camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably they were experienced, and they did such a job. I'll never forget it. And, uh, so I stayed here for about-- till around September, and then they used to send--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Wels?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wels, yes. And then they used to send lists from each camp to people who are there. And I found a cousin of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a displaced camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Displaced camp, yeah. And [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They used to call it the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It used to be for the, uh-- you know, for the soldiers, and they kept us there. And they fed us nice, and they took care of us. And then I decided I want to go. And my cousin that I saw on the list, she was living in Bergen-Belsen. So I says, well, we got together a few people, a few youngsters, and we traveled up to Bergen-Belsen. But in the mean time, the high holidays came along. And we stopped in Felderfink, and we spent the high holidays in Felderfink.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also a displaced camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also displaced camp. And we, uh-- and now from there, after the holidays, we traveled up to Bergen-Belsen. And from there, I-- I, uh, met my cousin. I met my cousin. It so happened that I came into the same barrack where my cousin was. And I was in a room with someone else that I knew before. And all of a sudden my cousin walks in-- she lives now in Montreal, actually-- walks in. I says, aren't you so-and-so? She says, aren't you? So therefore, we got together, and we stood together ever since, for a long-- for a few years. And then she got married, and I met my wife, and my first wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name was Rosa, and she came also not far from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: She a survivor, yes. She was a survivor. And we met, and we dated. And then in 1949, we decided we're going to go to Israel because her-- her father, as a matter of fact, survived. He was the oldest 55-year-old man in the whole district that he came back home from camp. And so therefore, we went to Israel, and we got married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at that time, it was in '49?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was already Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already Israel, yes, because in 1948, Israel was for-- was formed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to Israel in 1949, and we stayed there for 2 and 1/2 years. And I just couldn't stand the, uh-- the heat and all that. I mean, I couldn't stand it. So we tried to get out of there. And then we got out to Canada, and we stayed in Canada for 12 and 1/2 years. And my daughter-- coming from Israel, coming to Canada, we had-- we had to stop in the consulate in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter was born in--

speaker: interviewee
text: My older daughter was born in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris, In 1951 December 31. And then we-- from there, we stayed another three weeks in Paris, and also the, uh, Rothschild Hospital. They were also very nice people. They treated my wife-- even though we didn't pay anything. We didn't have any money actually. But they treated us very nicely. And from there, we emigrated to Canada. And we stayed there for 12 and 1/2 years. And my younger daughter was born there in 1956. And then my wife has a sister here in Los Angeles, and we decided to come here to Los Angeles. And we're living pretty happily here, and my wife passed away unfortunate in 19-- in 1989. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, I want to track back. Do you think because you were not well-to-do and because you were used to going to without shoes, and you were used to you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hard life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's what you mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: That might have helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's what helped me survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think this little bit of, because you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to hard life. Because I remember, the people used to come, and they-- I remember in Plaszow even, I was working with my uncle at the [? shoes, ?] and there was a doctor from Satmar, a doctor-- I'll never forget that-- a doctor. He walked by, and he says, I need work. But they didn't need no doctors there, so he put him into labors there, in hard labor. And he says, I wish I would be a shoemaker. That was his expression. He walked by, and he says, I wish I would be a shoemaker. And I probably would stay alive. Otherwise, I won't be able to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and father died before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Natural cause. Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Natural. Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still don't know-- yeah, I want to bring back-- and also when I was in Gusen Zwei, I had a meister, you know a meister, a--

speaker: interviewer
text: The head of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: A civilian from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow he took a liking to me or something. He used to bring me sometimes a slice of bread, hidden away.

speaker: interviewer
text: That helped too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and a potato, sometimes a cooked potato. And one time I had the nerve to ask this man, I says to this man, I was a poor boy at home. I was an orphan. I never done anything wrong to nobody. I hardly survived. Why? Why do I have to pay a price like this? Just because I'm Jewish? Is that my fault that I was born Jewish? It's not my fault. But I-- I-- I-- I-- I have just asked you why. I'm asking you why. Why am I here? What have I done wrong? What? Just walked away. Didn't say anything.
